text: Perioperative and functional results of robot-assisted radical cystectomy with totally intracorporeal neobladder. The VESPA technique. Ideal stage IIa report.

As compared to the original VASPA description, the ReFind technique used a forty centimeters ileal loop which is entirely detubularized without the five centimeters proximal segment closed which was present in the original description.

The surgeon performs robot-assisted reticulocystectomy and lymph node dissection, focusing on preserving a flap of visceral peritoneum. This flap is sutured distally to the periurethral tissue using a two-needle, three-o-fill block, providing hemostasis on the cystectomy pad and supporting the posterior wall of the neobladder.

The surgeon begins the neobladder configuration by performing an anastomosis of the ileal loop to the urethral stump using the Wald-Wendhoven technique. A two-needle, 3-0 Vicryl suture is applied over an 18 Fr Foley catheter, with additional 3-0 Monocryl sutures used for reinforcement if necessary.

After completing the anastomosis, the surgeon places two ten-centimeter strips of Tabotamp proximally and distally to the urethral ileal anastomosis to mark the first twenty centimeters of the ileal loop. A third twenty-centimeter Tabotamp strip is placed cranially to mark the last twenty centimeters of the ileal loop, with a three ovicryl stay suture securing the position at this mark.

The surgeon performs ileal resection and lateral-lateral ileal anastomosis using Echelon-Flex-Sixty endopath staplers. Indocyanine green is applied to assess the vascularization of the ileo-ileal anastomosis and ureters. The isolated forty centimeters ileal loop is fully detubularized along the anti-mesenteric border.

After completing the detubularization of the ileal loop, the surgeon attaches the stay suture at the twenty-centimeter mark to the lateral abdominal wall using a hemlock clip. The first ileal folding is then performed by aligning the medial margins at the forty-centimeter and twenty-centimeter marks.

The surgeon uses a two-needle, three-o-fill block suture to perform two semi-continuous sutures on the ileal loop, with one suture running cranially toward the thirty centimeters mark and the other running caudally toward the ten centimeters mark.

The surgeon performs the second ileal folding, aligning the medial margins at the thirty centimeters and zero centimeter marks. A two-needle, three-ofil block suture is used to create two semi-continuous sutures, beginning in the middle of the suture line and extending cranially toward the zero centimeter mark and caudally toward the ten centimeters mark.

The surgeon performs a direct anastomosis of the left ureter to the neobladder using three semi-continuous sutures with real monosyn sutures. A six-sherier urethral catheter is placed following the anastomosis. The same anastomosis procedure is then performed on the right ureter.

The surgeon performs the final folding to close the neobladder using a two-needle, three-ofil block suture. Two semi-continuous sutures are applied, starting at the median line and extending towards the site of ureter reimplantation.

The surgeon uses the final parts of the last two sutures to anchor the remnants of the peritoneum to the neobladder, making the ureteric reimplantations extraperitoneal and securing the ilioilial anastomosis to the lateral abdominal wall.

This is the final aspect of the Vespa neobladder at two weeks cystogram. We reported our initial experience with the robot assisted reticle cystectomy and intracorporeal neobladder according to the Vespa refinement technique.

Our technique offers the advantages of being easy to perform, includes only intracorporeal suturing and no use of a stapler to reconfigure the neobladder. Moreover, it does not have a funnel configuration of the bladder neck and the ureters are implanted on their respective sides. We experienced an extremely low prevalence of high-grade complications, clear surgical margins in all cases, acceptable oncological outcomes, and fair functional outcomes at the available follow-up.
